title: localization — helper functions
description: Helper overrides + our own `t()` short helper. All Laravel behaviours preserved because our decorator EXTENDS Illuminate\Translation\Translator rather than replacing it.

helpers.purpose: Short helper. Equivalent to Laravel's __() but delegates to our decorated Translator. Returns string OR array OR pluralised form depending on the key + params.
behaviours: Array-return: t('validation.custom') → array (Laravel returns arrays as-is for structured keys)
Count → plural: t('items', ['count' => 5]) → uses trans_choice under the hood when the value contains a `|` pluralisation marker
Object attribute interpolation: t('user.:field', ['field' => 'name']) → interpolates then re-resolves
Locale override: t('users.title', [], 'fr') → forces French regardless of active locale
JSON files: t('Welcome, :name') → reads lang/en.json (Laravel's built-in JSON translation behaviour, plus DB overrides)
helpers.flow: 1) Check Redis LRU cache. 2) Query Translation DB (workspace, then platform). 3) Read from file translation (lang/*). 4) Fallback locale + repeat. 5) Auto-translate if enabled. 6) Return raw key (Laravel default).

helpers.purpose: Laravel's built-in helper. Our decorator handles the resolution transparently — nothing to change in existing code.
Laravel's helper. When called without arguments, returns the translator instance. Otherwise resolves the key.
behaviours: trans() with no args → the Translator instance for advanced use
Simple: trans_choice('items', 5) → uses key's `|` splits: `{0} None|{1} One|[2,*] :count`

helpers.purpose: Ad-hoc translation of dynamic strings that aren't part of the translation catalogue. Consumes AI-translation entitlement.
behaviours: Result cached in Translation table with namespace='dynamic', key=SHA-256 of source text
Refuses if entitlement quota exhausted (returns source text unchanged + logs warning)

notes: We do NOT wrap or replace Laravel's helpers. We bind our Translator subclass as the 'translator' service, so `__()` and `trans()` and `@lang()` in Blade all route through our decorator.
Our `t()` is a convenience alias. Nothing prevents workspaces from continuing to use `__()`.

examples.title: Pluralisation
